_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: File I/O practice 1
questions.prompt: Which type represents a file-system path?
choices: Path
FileText
Scanner
StringBuilder
questions.explanation: `Path` represents a location and can be passed to Files operations. A String contains characters but does not provide the file-system abstraction.

questions.title: File I/O practice 2
choices: It closes the stream automatically
It sorts every line
It prevents IOException at compile time
It converts the file to an array
questions.explanation: The stream holds an open file resource. Try-with-resources calls its close method even when processing throws an exception.

questions.title: Trace File I/O
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The same Path is used for writing and reading. UTF-8 makes the text encoding an explicit part of the file format. The resulting output is 'CS101\nMATH120'.

questions.title: File I/O good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for File I/O?
choices: Represent locations with Path.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Represent locations with Path. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: File I/O practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices: Files.writeString(path, "next\n");
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: The left side replaces existing text. The right side adds content at the end by passing the append option.

questions.title: File I/O practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Default writing replaces contents'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
A successful write can still be the wrong action. If old records matter, include the append option or read, update, and rewrite the complete data intentionally.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: A successful write can still be the wrong action. If old records matter, include the append option or read, update, and rewrite the complete data intentionally.

questions.title: File I/O practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for File I/O?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Select UTF-8 for portable text.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Select UTF-8 for portable text. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: File I/O practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for File I/O?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Decide whether a write replaces or appends.
questions.explanation: Decide whether a write replaces or appends. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: File I/O practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for File I/O?
choices: Handle IOException at a layer that can respond.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Handle IOException at a layer that can respond. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: File I/O practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for File I/O?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Stream large files and close the stream automatically.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Stream large files and close the stream automatically. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
